clusters.title: Scientists discover surprising brain trigger behind high blood pressure
clusters.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising brain-based trigger for high blood pressure, tracing it to a small region in the brainstem that normally controls breathing. This area, which kicks in during forceful exhalations like coughing, laughing, or exercise, also appears to activate nerves that tighten blood vessels—raising blood pressure. When researchers switched off this region in experiments, blood pressure dropped back to normal, suggesting it plays a direct role in hypertension.

items.title: Scientists discover surprising brain trigger behind high blood pressure
items.description: Scientists have uncovered a surprising brain-based trigger for high blood pressure, tracing it to a small region in the brainstem that normally controls breathing. This area, which kicks in during forceful exhalations like coughing, laughing, or exercise, also appears to activate nerves that tighten blood vessels—raising blood pressure. When researchers switched off this region in experiments, blood pressure dropped back to normal, suggesting it plays a direct role in hypertension.

clusters.title: How aggressive breast cancer turns off the immune system
clusters.summary: Researchers are launching a new project to crack the mystery of aggressive breast cancer, where predicting disease progression remains a major hurdle. By studying how tumors interact with and suppress the immune system, scientists aim to identify new biomarkers that reveal how the cancer evolves. Using real patient samples, the team hopes to turn earlier discoveries into practical clinical tools.

items.title: How aggressive breast cancer turns off the immune system
items.description: Researchers are launching a new project to crack the mystery of aggressive breast cancer, where predicting disease progression remains a major hurdle. By studying how tumors interact with and suppress the immune system, scientists aim to identify new biomarkers that reveal how the cancer evolves. Using real patient samples, the team hopes to turn earlier discoveries into practical clinical tools. The goal: more precise, personalized treatments that can outsmart even the most dangerous tumors.

clusters.title: Listening to bad music makes you crave sugar, study finds
clusters.summary: An experimental study found that listening to disliked music decreases a person's general desire to eat, but specifically increases their preference for high-sugar foods, likely as a way to cope with the negative mood induced by the audio.

items.title: Listening to bad music makes you crave sugar, study finds
items.description: An experimental study found that listening to disliked music decreases a person's general desire to eat, but specifically increases their preference for high-sugar foods, likely as a way to cope with the negative mood induced by the audio.

clusters.title: Study on western diet, binge drinking, and liver disease
clusters.summary: Four undergraduate students who will each receive their diploma at next month's Commencement ceremony at the University of Vermont are all leaving with more than their degrees, as they are credited as co-authors on a newly published, peer-reviewed study examining how a Western diet combined with binge drinking accelerates liver disease—a notable achievement for undergraduate researchers.

items.title: Study on western diet, binge drinking, and liver disease
items.description: Four undergraduate students who will each receive their diploma at next month's Commencement ceremony at the University of Vermont are all leaving with more than their degrees, as they are credited as co-authors on a newly published, peer-reviewed study examining how a Western diet combined with binge drinking accelerates liver disease—a notable achievement for undergraduate researchers.

clusters.title: Scientists find hidden brain cells helping deadly cancer grow
clusters.summary: Scientists in Canada have uncovered a surprising weakness in glioblastoma, one of the deadliest brain cancers. They found that certain brain cells—once believed to only support healthy nerves—can actually help tumors grow by sending signals that strengthen cancer cells. When researchers blocked this communication, tumor growth slowed dramatically in lab models.

items.title: Scientists find hidden brain cells helping deadly cancer grow

They found that certain brain cells—once believed to only support healthy nerves—can actually help tumors grow by sending signals that strengthen cancer cells. When researchers blocked this communication, tumor growth slowed dramatically in lab models.

clusters.title: Forget daily pills. This shot works when blood pressure meds fail
clusters.summary: A twice-yearly injection may soon change how high blood pressure is treated. In a global trial, patients receiving the experimental drug zilebesiran alongside standard therapy saw greater blood pressure reductions than those on standard treatment alone. The drug works by blocking a key liver protein, helping blood vessels relax.

items.title: Forget daily pills. This shot works when blood pressure meds fail
items.description: A twice-yearly injection may soon change how high blood pressure is treated. In a global trial, patients receiving the experimental drug zilebesiran alongside standard therapy saw greater blood pressure reductions than those on standard treatment alone. The drug works by blocking a key liver protein, helping blood vessels relax. Researchers say this long-lasting approach could make it much easier for patients to keep their condition under control.

clusters.title: Scientists solve 30-year mystery of a hidden nutrient that protects the brain and fights cancer
clusters.summary: Scientists have finally uncovered the missing link in how our bodies absorb queuosine, a rare micronutrient crucial for brain health, memory, stress response, and cancer defense. For decades, researchers suspected a transporter had to exist, but it remained elusive—until now. By identifying the gene SLC35F2 as the gateway into cells, this breakthrough opens new possibilities for therapies and highlights how diet and gut microbes profoundly shape human health.

items.title: Scientists solve 30-year mystery of a hidden nutrient that protects the brain and fights cancer

clusters.title: Listen: Cheap Health Insurance Isn’t Always Cheap
clusters.summary: Across the country, people are choosing lower monthly premiums in exchange for higher out-of-pocket risk. Reporter Jackie Fortiér explains what the shift means for Americans’ health and wallets.

items.title: Listen: Cheap Health Insurance Isn’t Always Cheap
items.description: Across the country, people are choosing lower monthly premiums in exchange for higher out-of-pocket risk. Reporter Jackie Fortiér explains what the shift means for Americans’ health and wallets.

clusters.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation.

items.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
items.description: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation. The finding could help pave the way for earlier diagnosis and more personalized treatments.

clusters.title: Social media posts reveal that women in the UK were repeatedly gaslit over womb procedure pain
clusters.summary: Women have described being dismissed, disbelieved, and left in serious pain during a routine womb examination that affects tens of thousands every year. Users of parenting forum Mumsnet who underwent hysteroscopy procedures also shared concerns about receiving unclear information before treatment and being given little or no pain relief afterward. Some women, who described feeling physically and emotionally vulnerable, compared the experience to sexual assault.

items.title: Social media posts reveal that women in the UK were repeatedly gaslit over womb procedure pain
items.description: Women have described being dismissed, disbelieved, and left in serious pain during a routine womb examination that affects tens of thousands every year. Users of parenting forum Mumsnet who underwent hysteroscopy procedures also shared concerns about receiving unclear information before treatment and being given little or no pain relief afterward. Some women, who described feeling physically and emotionally vulnerable, compared the experience to sexual assault. Now, research led by the University of Reading, published in the journal Women's Health, has analyzed 4,644 posts from Mumsnet users posted between 2018 and 2024.

clusters.title: This new therapy turns off pain without opioids or addiction
clusters.summary: Scientists have developed a new gene therapy that quiets pain at its source in the brain—without the addictive risks of opioids. Using AI to map how pain is processed, they created a targeted “off switch” that mimics morphine’s benefits but skips its dangerous side effects. In early tests, it delivered lasting relief without affecting normal sensations.

items.title: This new therapy turns off pain without opioids or addiction
items.description: Scientists have developed a new gene therapy that quiets pain at its source in the brain—without the addictive risks of opioids. Using AI to map how pain is processed, they created a targeted “off switch” that mimics morphine’s benefits but skips its dangerous side effects. In early tests, it delivered lasting relief without affecting normal sensations. The discovery could mark a major step toward safer, non-addictive pain treatments.

clusters.title: Brain scans reveal how ketamine quickly lifts severe depression
clusters.summary: A new brain-imaging study has revealed how ketamine produces its fast antidepressant effects in people with treatment-resistant depression. Researchers tracked changes in a critical brain receptor that helps neurons communicate and found that ketamine reshapes its activity in specific brain regions tied to mood and reward. These shifts strongly matched improvements in patients’ symptoms.

items.title: Brain scans reveal how ketamine quickly lifts severe depression
items.description: A new brain-imaging study has revealed how ketamine produces its fast antidepressant effects in people with treatment-resistant depression. Researchers tracked changes in a critical brain receptor that helps neurons communicate and found that ketamine reshapes its activity in specific brain regions tied to mood and reward. These shifts strongly matched improvements in patients’ symptoms. The findings could help scientists develop better ways to predict who will benefit from ketamine therapy.

clusters.title: Extreme climate events increase heart disease risk, new study reveals
clusters.summary: A novel study has revealed a link between extreme weather and the risk of cardiovascular disease among middle-aged and older adults in 157 Chinese cities. Based on a city's climate and location, exposure to extreme heat, cold, and precipitation each increases the risk of heart disease.

items.title: Extreme climate events increase heart disease risk, new study reveals
items.description: A novel study has revealed a link between extreme weather and the risk of cardiovascular disease among middle-aged and older adults in 157 Chinese cities. Based on a city's climate and location, exposure to extreme heat, cold, and precipitation each increases the risk of heart disease.

clusters.title: Brain study reveals hidden link between autism and ADHD
clusters.summary: Scientists are uncovering a surprising connection between autism and ADHD that goes deeper than labels. Instead of diagnoses, it’s the severity of autism-like traits that seems to shape how the brain is wired—even in children who don’t officially have autism. The study found that certain brain networks tied to thinking and social behavior stay unusually connected in kids with stronger autism symptoms, hinting at a different developmental path.

items.title: Brain study reveals hidden link between autism and ADHD

clusters.title: Research finds that streamlining the benefits application process increases WIC application, enrollment rates
clusters.summary: Being stuck in a bureaucratic morass is frustrating and time-consuming; known as administrative burden, this phenomenon is unfortunately a regular feature of modern life. For some, however, administrative burden is more than an annoyance; it's an obstacle to getting essential government benefits and assistance. Because the process can be so confusing, opaque, and difficult, many people end up not signing up for the help they need for themselves and their children, even though they are eligible.

items.title: Research finds that streamlining the benefits application process increases WIC application, enrollment rates

clusters.title: Cannabis study finds THC can create false memories
clusters.summary: THC doesn’t just blur memories—it can create new ones that never happened. In a controlled experiment, cannabis users were much more likely to recall words that were never shown and struggled with tasks like remembering to do something later. Researchers found that THC disrupted many different memory systems at once.

items.title: Cannabis study finds THC can create false memories
items.description: THC doesn’t just blur memories—it can create new ones that never happened. In a controlled experiment, cannabis users were much more likely to recall words that were never shown and struggled with tasks like remembering to do something later. Researchers found that THC disrupted many different memory systems at once. Surprisingly, moderate doses caused memory problems similar to higher doses.

clusters.title: Combining cannabis with opioids offers no added pain relief for knee arthritis patients, study concludes
clusters.summary: Combining cannabis with an opioid did not improve acute pain for people with knee arthritis, according to results of a study published in Anesthesiology.

items.title: Combining cannabis with opioids offers no added pain relief for knee arthritis patients, study concludes
items.description: Combining cannabis with an opioid did not improve acute pain for people with knee arthritis, according to results of a study published in Anesthesiology.

clusters.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine
clusters.summary: Dragonflies may see the world in a way that pushes beyond human limits—and surprisingly, they do it using the same molecular trick we evolved ourselves. Scientists discovered that these insects can detect extremely deep red light, even edging into near-infrared, thanks to a specialized visual protein strikingly similar to the one in human eyes. This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

items.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine

This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

clusters.title: Scientists crack an 'undruggable' childhood cancer protein, opening a path to treatments for neuroblastoma
clusters.summary: Researchers at Linköping University show how two important cancer-related proteins can be prevented from collaborating with each other. The discovery shows the way toward future medications to combat, for example, neuroblastoma in children. Their study has been published in the journal Nature Communications.

clusters.title: In Connecticut, Doctors Now Sue Patients Most Over Medical Bills, Surpassing Hospitals
clusters.summary: Physicians, dentists, and other nonhospital providers account for more than 80% of health care debt collection cases in Connecticut courts, a CT Mirror-KFF Health News investigation finds.
